items.title: The Space Between
items.text:
Welcome to my personal website for keeping and sharing notes, learnings and tips I've picked up along the way.

This is a collection of explainers, tutorials, quick notes (mostly for my brain to understand) and code snippets on concepts I've found useful to formalise.

items.title: What is Git?
items.text:
Git is a piece of software on your machine used to track changes in files. Git let's us:

  • See what's changed, when and by whom
  • Compare different versions of your work
  • Collaborate with other people without overwriting each other's changes
  • Maintain a complete audit trail of a project
  • Revert mistakes

items.title: Key Terms
items.text: Term Definition Repository (Repo) A project tracked by Git Commit A snapshot of changes at a point in time Branch An isolated line of development Merge Combining changes from different branches Clone Creating a local copy of a repository Push Uploading changes to a remote repository Pull Downloading changes from a remote repository

items.title: Why Should I Use Git?
items.text:
Git is good for tracking changes with:

  • Scripts (Python,R,SQL)
  • Notebooks (Rmd, Jupyter Notebooks)
  • FME workspaces
  • Configuration files
  • Documentation

items.title: 1. Change History
items.text:
Every modification is recorded with:

  • What changed
  • Who changed it
  • When it changed
  • Why it changed (through commit messages - free text from the author)

items.title: 2. Rollbacks
items.text: Git allows you to quickly return to a previous working version without manually restoring files.

items.title: 3. Safe Experimentation
items.text: Create a branch and experiment freely without altering the main/production branch.

items.title: 4. Better Collaboration
items.text: Git solves the classic problem of multiple people editing the same files. Git helps coordinate changes.

items.title: Git and GitHub
items.text:
Question

"What's the difference between Git and GitHub?"

Git is the actual version control software installed on your machine.

It manages version history, branches, commits and merges.

Git works perfectly well without an internet connection.

GitHub is a cloud platform built around Git.

It is a platform where repositories can be hosted, developers can collaborate, Pull Requests (PRs) and Issues can be rasied and tracked and other Continuous Integration /Continuous Development (CI/CD) apabilities.

An analogy:

Git GitHub Engine Car Version control technology Collaboration platform Works locally Lives online Tracks changes Helps teams work together

Other Git hosting platforms exist too:

  • GitHub
  • Azure DevOps
  • GitLab
  • Bitbucket

items.title: Standard Workflow
items.text:
Clone Repository
       ↓
Create Branch
       ↓
Make Changes
       ↓
Commit Changes
       ↓
Push Branch
       ↓
Open Pull Request
       ↓
Code Review
       ↓
Merge into Main

items.title: Git Flow
items.text: A branching strategy that defines how code moves from development into production.

items.title: main
items.text:
Contains production-ready code.

Anything in main should be QA'ed, stable and deployable.

items.title: develop
items.text:
Contains the latest development work.

Features are merged here before eventually being released.

items.title: Feature Branches
items.text:
Used for developing new functionality.

Example:

feature/add-tracking-module
feature/add-health-classification

items.title: Release Branches
items.text:
Created when preparing a release.

Used for:

  • Final testing
  • Documentation updates
  • Minor fixes

Example:

release/v2.0

items.title: Hotfix Branches
items.text:
Created from production when urgent issues occur.

Example:

hotfix/fix-critical-bug

items.title: Git Flow Diagram
items.text:
main
 │
 ├───────────────┐
 │               │
 │           hotfix/*
 │               │
 │               ▼
 └───────────────●

develop
 │
 ├── feature/*
 ├── feature/*
 └── feature/*

       │
       ▼

    release/*
       │
       ▼

      main
Question

When Should You Use Git Flow?

Git Flow works well when:

  • Multiple developers contribute
  • Formal releases are required
  • Production stability is important

For smaller projects or on projects where you are the sole developer, simpler workflows such as are often faster and easier to manage.

items.title: Setup
items.text:
Initialise a new repository:

git init

Clone an existing repository:

git clone <repository-url>

Check your Git version:

git --version

items.title: Commonly Used
items.text:
Check repository status:

git status

See recent commits:

git log

Add all changed files:

git add .

Add a specific file:

git add myfile.py

Create a commit:

git commit -m "Add feature X"

items.title: Branching
items.text:
View branches:

git branch

Create a new branch:

git checkout -b feature/new-feature

Switch branches:

git checkout develop

Merge a branch:

git merge feature/new-feature

Delete a branch:

git branch -d feature/new-feature

items.title: Pushing and Pulling
items.text:
Download latest changes:

git pull

Upload commits:

git push

Push a new branch:

git push -u origin feature/new-feature

items.title: Comparing Changes
items.text:
Compare branches:

git diff main develop

items.title: Undoing Mistakes
items.text:
Undo changes to a file:

git checkout -- myfile.py

Unstage a file:

git reset myfile.py

Revert a commit safely:

git revert <commit-id>

items.title: The "Save My Work Quickly" Commands
items.text:
Temporarily save changes:

git stash

Restore stashed changes:

git stash pop

items.title: "Do the dance"
items.text:
This is my favourite. Used when I need to update branch without losing local changes: 

git stash #this stashes the changes in my current branch
git checkout main #checks out the main branch
git pull 
git checkout myoriginalbranch
git stash pop 
git merge main #brings in all the changes from main into current branch

items.title: Load Libraries
items.text:
We will be using a few libraries with good built-in functions for reading in images. 

import cv2
import numpy as np
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
import imageio
import numpy as np
from PIL import Image
from scipy.ndimage import map_coordinates

items.title: Loading in an image
items.text:
We will use a dummy 360 - something I've just randomly found on the internet. Image was taken from NOIRLab.

We can read this in as a numpy array using cv2 

path_to_image = r"assets/example360image.jpg"

#read in the image using cv2
image_360 = cv2.imread(path_to_image)

#here, we need to first set the image colour format for matplotlib as it's curently BGR. We need it to be RGB
image_360 = cv2.cvtColor(image_360, cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB)

#plot
plt.imshow(image_360)

items.title: Shape of the image
items.text:
image_360 is a numpy array, and it has 3 values in it's shape. We can inspect it using the .shape method. 

print(image_360.shape)  # (height, width, channels)
The output from above should be (300, 600, 3), which means the image is 300 pixels tall, 600 pixels wide and there are 3 channels in the image.

The 3 channels mean that each pixel has three colour channels (Red, Green and Blue). Each channel will have it's own value from 0 to 255, representing the intensity of that colour in the pixel.

items.title: Cropping an image (no correction for warp)
items.text:
We can crop the image as is, without correcting for warp.

Suppose we want to divide the image into 4 quadrants: Top left, top right, bottom left and bottom right. 

quadrants = [
    (0, 0, image_360.shape[1] // 2, image_360.shape[0] // 2),  # Top-left
    (image_360.shape[1] // 2, 0, image_360.shape[1], image_360.shape[0] // 2),  # Top-right
    (0, image_360.shape[0] // 2, image_360.shape[1] // 2, image_360.shape[0]),  # Bottom-left
    (image_360.shape[1] // 2, image_360.shape[0] // 2, image_360.shape[1], image_360.shape[0]),  # Bottom-right
]

To visualise the quadrants on the image, we can run the below 

#draw the quadrant lines on the image
plt.imshow(image_360)
for (x1, y1, x2, y2) in quadrants:
    plt.plot([x1, x2], [y1, y1], color='red', linewidth=2)  # Top line
    plt.plot([x1, x2], [y2, y2], color='red', linewidth=2)  # Bottom line
    plt.plot([x1, x1], [y1, y2], color='red', linewidth=2)  # Left line
    plt.plot([x2, x2], [y1, y2], color='red', linewidth=2)  # Right line

plt.show()

Let's crop the image according to the quadrants that we created above. 

#divide the image into 4 quadrants and display
quadrants = [
    (0, 0, image_360.shape[1] // 2, image_360.shape[0] // 2),  # Top-left
    (image_360.shape[1] // 2, 0, image_360.shape[1], image_360.shape[0] // 2),  # Top-right
    (0, image_360.shape[0] // 2, image_360.shape[1] // 2, image_360.shape[0]),  # Bottom-left
    (image_360.shape[1] // 2, image_360.shape[0] // 2, image_360.shape[1], image_360.shape[0]),  # Bottom-right
]   


q1_crop = image_360[quadrants[0][1]:quadrants[0][3], quadrants[0][0]:quadrants[0][2]]
q2_crop = image_360[quadrants[1][1]:quadrants[1][3], quadrants[1][0]:quadrants[1][2]]
q3_crop = image_360[quadrants[2][1]:quadrants[2][3], quadrants[2][0]:quadrants[2][2]]
q4_crop = image_360[quadrants[3][1]:quadrants[3][3], quadrants[3][0]:quadrants[3][2]]

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 10))
plt.subplot(2, 2, 1)
plt.imshow(q1_crop)
plt.title("Top-left Quadrant")
plt.subplot(2, 2, 2)
plt.imshow(q2_crop)
plt.title("Top-right Quadrant")
plt.subplot(2, 2, 3)
plt.imshow(q3_crop)
plt.title("Bottom-left Quadrant")
plt.subplot(2, 2, 4)
plt.imshow(q4_crop)
plt.title("Bottom-right Quadrant")
plt.show()

items.title: Bounding Box Formats
items.text:
Bounding box coordinates will come in two main forms: - XYXY: Top left X and Y coordinates, bottom right X and Y coordinates of the box. - XYWH: The top left X and Y coordinates (origin) and the width and height of the box.

These can also come in absolute coordinate format(where the coordinates are relative to the true pixel position in the image), or normalised format (where the coordinates are normalised to the width and height of the image and go from 0 to 1).

Format Absolute Normalised XYXY [649, 671, 775, 1126] [0.3382475845116023, 0.3498533770268592, 0.40376361865331395, 0.5866210660303719] XYWH [712.0, 898.5, 126, 455] [0.37083333333333335, 0.46796875, 0.065625, 0.23697916666666666]

To go from XYXY to XYWH, we can use the following function 

def xyxy_to_xywh(box):
    """
    Convert bounding box from (x1, y1, x2, y2) format to (x, y, w, h) format.

    Parameters:
    - box (list of float): The bounding box coordinates as a list [x1, y1, x2, y2].

    Returns:
    - list of float: The bounding box coordinates in (x, y, w, h) format.
    """
    x1, y1, x2, y2 = box
    w = x2 - x1
    h = y2 - y1
    x = x1 + w / 2
    y = y1 + h / 2
    return [x, y, w, h]

#Suppose our bounding box has an absolute coordinates in XYXY of: 
box_xyxy = [649, 671, 775, 1126]

box_xywh = xyxy_to_xywh(box) # [712.0, 898.5, 126, 455]

items.title: Drawing the bounding box on the image
items.text:
Let's say we have a bounding box with the following absolute coordinates that we would like to draw on the image [188, 13, 581, 164]. We can draw it directly using cv2. 

bbox_xyxy = [188, 13, 581, 164]

plt.imshow(image_360)

#using cv2.rectangle and drawing the coordinates directly on it. Colour specify red, and thickness of 5. 
cv2.rectangle(image_360, (box_xyxy[0], box_xyxy[1]), (box_xyxy[2], box_xyxy[3]), (255, 0, 0), 5)

plt.show()

items.title: Stats shouldn't be scary!
items.text: Some text here

items.title: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics
items.text: Recently, I worked on several projects where we used bayesian models for prediction. I've had to explain bayesian statistics to many stakeholders (with varying degrees of compleixty) so I thought it would be helpful to formalise this into a little crash course.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Introduction
items.text:
Bayesian statistics provides a framework for updating our beliefs as new evidence becomes available.

Rather than treating parameters as fixed but unknown values, Bayesian methods treat them as probabilities that can change as new information is observed.

The key question for Bayesian stats is:

Question

"Given what I already know, and given the new evidence I have collected, what should I believe now?"
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: How is Bayesian Different to Frequentist Statistics?
items.text: The main difference is how probability is interpreted.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Frequentist Statistics
items.text:
Frequentist statistics views probability as the long-run frequency of events.

For example:

Question

"If we repeated an experiment thousands of times, how often would a particular outcome occur?"

Unknown quantities (such as a regression coefficient or population mean) are considered fixed values. We estimate them using data and quantify uncertainty through confidence intervals and p-values.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Bayesian Statistics
items.text:
Bayesian statistics views probability as a degree of belief.

For example:

Question

"Given what I know right now, how likely is it that this statement is true?"

Unknown quantities are represented as probability distributions rather than fixed values.

As new data becomes available, our beliefs are updated.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Example: Smoking and Lung Cancer
items.text:
Imagine you have two patients: - Patient A: Never smoked - Patient B: Chian smoked for 40 years.

Before we conduct any medical tests, which patient do you think you'd be more concerned about just based on those two pieces of information? Intuitively, you would say Patient B immediately. This is because we already know that smoking is a major risk factor for lung cancer, so this existing knowledge forms our prior belief.

Suppose both patients go for scan which scans for lung cancer. The scan provides us with new evidence.

So, bayesian statistics combines what we already know (patiet's smoking history) and new evidence we have just observed (scan results) to calculate an updated probability for lung cancer.

  • A Frequentist asks: "If someone has lung cancer, how often would this scan produce a positive result"
  • A Bayesian asks: "Given that this patient is a long-term smoker and has a positive scan, what is the probability they have lung cancer?"
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Comparing the Two Approaches
items.text: Question Frequentist Bayesian What is probability? Long-run frequency Degree of belief Parameters Fixed but unknown Probability distributions Prior knowledge Not formally included Explicitly incorporated Typical outputs p-values, confidence intervals Posterior distributions, credible intervals Interpretation Probability of the data Probability of the hypothesis
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Bayes' Theorem
items.text:
Bayesian inference is built upon Bayes' Theorem:

\[ P(A \mid B)=\frac{P(B \mid A)P(A)}{P(B)} \]

Where:

Term Meaning \(P(A)\) Prior belief \(P(B \mid A)\) Likelihood of observing the evidence \(P(B)\) Probability of observing the evidence \(P(A \mid B)\) Updated belief (Posterior)

Often summarised as:

\[ \text{Posterior} \propto \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Prior} \]
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Bayesian Updating
items.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Prior Belief]
    B[New Evidence]
    C[Bayes' Theorem]
    D[Posterior Belief]

    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> D

The posterior from one analysis can become the prior for the next, allowing knowledge to accumulate over time.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Example 1: Predicting the Presence of a Species in an Area
items.text: Suppose an we are assessing whether a rare bat species is present within a woodland.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Prior Knowledge
items.text:
Based on historical surveys in similar habitats, we know that:

Probability the species is present = 70%

Prior = 0.70
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: New Evidence
items.text:
An acoustic detector records several calls matching the target species.

Previous validation studies show:

If the species is present, the detector identifies it correctly 90% of the time.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Bayesian Update
items.text:
The new evidence increases our confidence that the species is present.

flowchart TD
    A[Habitat Information]
    B[Prior Probability: 70%]
    C[Acoustic Survey Results]
    D[Updated Probability of Presence]

    A --> B
    B --> D
    C --> D

Unlike a simple "present" or "absent" classification, Bayesian analysis provides a probability that reflects both historical knowledge and newly collected evidence.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Example 2: Predicting Voter Choice
items.text: Imagine we want to predict which UK political party a voter is most likely to support.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Prior Beliefs
items.text:
Before speaking to the voter, we might use national polling data:

Party Prior Probability Labour 40% Conservative 25% Liberal Democrat 15% Green 10% Other 10%
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: New Evidence
items.text:
The voter tells us:

"Climate change is my most important issue."

Historically, voters who prioritise climate issues are more likely to support the Green Party or Labour than the Conservatives.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Posterior Beliefs
items.text:
After updating our beliefs:

Party Updated Probability Labour 45% Conservative 10% Liberal Democrat 15% Green 25% Other 5%
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Bayesian Voting Example
items.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Polling Data]
    B[Prior Party Probabilities]

    C[Voter Prioritises Climate Change]

    D[Updated Party Probabilities]

    A --> B
    B --> D
    C --> D

Bayesian inference combines existing knowledge with new evidence to produce a revised estimate of what is most likely.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Limitations of Bayesian Statistics
items.text: Bayesian methods are powerful, but they are not always the best choice.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: 1. Priors Can Be Subjective
items.text:
Bayesian models require prior assumptions.

Different analysts may choose different priors, which can lead to different conclusions, particularly when little data is available.

flowchart LR
    A[Analyst 1 Prior]
    B[Same Data]
    C[Posterior A]

    D[Analyst 2 Prior]
    E[Posterior B]

    A --> B
    B --> C

    D --> B
    B --> E

When datasets are small, results may be strongly influenced by these prior assumptions.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: 2. Computationally Intensive
items.text:
Many Bayesian models require simulation methods such as:

  • Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)
  • Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC)
  • Variational Inference

These can be significantly more computationally expensive than equivalent frequentist methods.

For large datasets or highly complex models, run times can become substantial.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: 3. Results Can Be Sensitive to Model Specification
items.text:
Bayesian models require decisions about:

  • Priors
  • Likelihood functions
  • Hierarchical structures

Poorly chosen assumptions can bias results, particularly when data is limited.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: 4. Frequentist Methods Are Often Simpler
items.text:
For many routine analyses:

  • Two-sample t-tests
  • Linear regression
  • ANOVA
  • Chi-squared tests

Frequentist approaches are often easier to implement, explain and reproduce.

If the objective is simply to estimate a parameter using a large dataset, a frequentist solution may be perfectly adequate.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Why Bayesian Statistics is Useful
items.text:
Despite its limitations, Bayesian statistics offers several compelling advantages:

  • Prior knowledge can be formally incorporated into analysis.
  • Probabilities are intuitive and easy to interpret.
  • Uncertainty is represented naturally through probability distributions.
  • Predictions can be continuously updated as new information becomes available.
  • Complex hierarchical and multi-level systems can be modelled effectively.
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Bayesian Learning Cycle
items.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Prior Knowledge]
    B[Collect Data]
    C[Calculate Posterior]
    D[Updated Understanding]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> A
path: A Crash Course in Bayesian Statistics

items.title: Bayesian Sequential Updating
items.text:
One of the most powerful features of Bayesian statistics is that it allows us to continuously update our beliefs as new evidence becomes available.

In Bayesian Sequential Updating, the posterior distribution from today becomes the prior distribution for tomorrow.

Rather than analysing all available data from scratch every time new information arrives, we update our existing beliefs with the latest evidence.

items.title: Key Intuition
items.text:
Bayesian inference follows a simple principle:

\[ \text{Posterior} \propto \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Prior} \]

When a new observation becomes available:

  1. Start with a prior belief.
  2. Collect new evidence.
  3. Calculate a posterior belief.
  4. Treat the posterior as the new prior.
  5. Repeat.

items.title: Sequential Updating Process
items.text:
flowchart LR

    A[Prior]
    B[New Data]
    C[Posterior]

    C --> D[New Prior]
    E[More Data]
    F[Updated Posterior]

    A --> C
    B --> C

    D --> F
    E --> F

items.title: Example: Smoking and Lung Cancer
items.text: I am diagnosing an individual for lung cancer.

items.title: Step 1: Prior Belief
items.text:
The individual is a chain smoker who has smoked for 40 years.

Based on historical evidence, we already believe:

This patient has a higher risk of lung cancer than the average person.

This forms the prior probability.

flowchart LR
    A[Smoking History]
    B[Prior Probability of Lung Cancer]

    A --> B

items.title: Step 2: New Evidence Arrives
items.text:
A chest X-ray reveals a suspicious shadow.

We can update our belief. 

flowchart LR
    A[Prior Risk]
    B[Chest X-ray Result]
    C[Updated Probability]

    A --> C
    B --> C

The resulting probability is called the posterior probability.

items.title: Step 3: More Evidence Arrives
items.text:
A CT scan is performed.

Instead of returning to the beginning and ignoring what has already been learned, we can use the previous posterior as the new prior.

flowchart LR
    A[Posterior After X-ray]
    B[CT Scan]
    C[New Posterior]

    A --> C
    B --> C

Risk estimates are refined further.

items.title: Step 4: A Biopsy Result Arrives
items.text:
Finally, a biopsy is performed.

Again, the previous posterior becomes the new prior.

flowchart LR
    A[Posterior After CT]
    B[Biopsy Results]
    C[Final Posterior Probability]

    A --> C
    B --> C

At each stage, the estimate becomes more informed because it incorporates all previous evidence.

items.title: Why Sequential Updating Is Useful
items.text:
Traditional statistical analysis often works like this:

Collect all data
↓
Run analysis
↓
Generate result

Bayesian Sequential Updating works differently:

Observation 1 → Update
Observation 2 → Update
Observation 3 → Update
Observation 4 → Update
...

This is good for when information arrives gradually over time, or we have varying degrees of confidence on our observations.

items.title: Ecology
items.text:
Suppose we want to determine whether a rare bat species is present.

Evidence may arrive in stages:

  1. Habitat assessment
  2. Acoustic recordings
  3. Emergence surveys
  4. eDNA sampling

Each survey incrementally updates our confidence in species presence.

items.title: Asset Management
items.text:
Sequential updating is particularly useful for infrastructure assets.

An asset may initially have a baseline health assessment.

Over time, new information arrives from:

  • Visual examinations
  • Train bourne monitoring systems
  • LiDAR surveys
  • Sensors
  • Failure records

Each observation can update the estimated asset condition rather than requiring a complete rebuild of the model.

items.title: Visualising Belief Updating
items.text:
A useful way to think about Bayesian updating is as a process of reducing uncertainty.

flowchart LR

    A[Very Uncertain Prior]
    B[Observation 1]
    C[Observation 2]
    D[Observation 3]
    E[High Confidence Posterior]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E

As more evidence accumulates:

  • Uncertainty decreases.
  • The posterior distribution becomes more concentrated (i.e., the distribution is less wide as we are more confident about where we'll end up).
  • Our estimates become more precise.

items.title: A Flapping Good Model: Collaborative approaches to machine learning for biodiversity
items.text: A talk given jointly with my colleague Sam Smith at GeoBusiness 2026 in London, UK.
path: A Flapping Good Model: Collaborative approaches to machine learning for biodiversity

items.title: Abstract
items.text:
Mott MacDonald showcase an innovative cross-disciplinary framework revolutionising habitat suitability modelling. Using advanced machine learning combined with ecological expertise, they’ve delivered a more robust, cost-effective survey and mitigation strategy for a major UK railway project.

With 1 million species threatened globally and 69% of UK species in decline, traditional approaches aren’t enough. Learn how their integrated INLA and CircuitScape methodology identifies critical habitat corridors, reduces project risks and costs, and delivers measurable benefits for both the client and nature.
path: A Flapping Good Model: Collaborative approaches to machine learning for biodiversity

items.title: Details
items.text:
Mott MacDonald have developed an innovative cross-disciplinary approach to habitat suitability modelling (HSM) that brings together expertise and innovation across the fields of geospatial, data science and ecology. By combining advanced machine learning with ecological expertise, we delivered a robust and actionable survey and mitigation strategy for a nationally significant linear infrastructure project in the UK. This presentation aims to showcase how we married advanced spatial data science and ecology to deliver a data-led, focussed and risk-based survey effort to inform design considerations of a major project from the outset – allowing us to design in permeability and connectivity, and design out impacts.

The climate crisis and biodiversity loss through development projects is at the forefront of project design, not least through the introduction of the Environment Act 2021 but also more recently through the Planning and Infrastructure Act 2025 . According to the IPBES Global Assessment, approximately 1 million species are threatened with extinction, with habitat fragmentation a primary driver. In the UK, 69% of species have declined since 1970 with transport infrastructure contributing significantly to habitat loss and fragmentation across landscapes. Today, drivers for development outpace any previously encountered. It is imperative that we develop innovative approaches that allow us to adopt systematic, transparent, robust and repeatable baseline assessment methods to identify areas for survey based on risk, challenge and define mitigation and provide data driven approaches to inform early discussions with stakeholders and design of major projects.

Ecological assessments attempt to understand how a development may impact the natural environment and inform necessary mitigations. Traditionally, these assessments have often operated in isolation from empirical and advanced modelling capabilities. Although there are a variety of statistical methods which can be used for modelling habitat suitability, striking the balance between ecological nuance needed for actionable outcomes and statistical robustness is difficult. This disconnects results in suboptimal decision-making, increased project risks and missed opportunities for a positive environmental impact.

We developed an unique framework and way of working, combining Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation (INLA), CircuitScape and ecological knowledge to predict bat species distributions across our project and the adjacent landscapes. Our approach allows us to quantitatively identify critical habitat corridors and potential fragmentation hotspots to ensure appropriate and justified mitigation is designed for the scheme.

In the presentation, we hope to show how a cross-disciplinary way of working delivers measurable benefits. First, we show how we built models that are both statistically robust and ecologically meaningful. We will also outline how our automated pipeline scales across large geographic areas and ingest a variety of datasets. Lastly, we’ll showcase how we use this data to drive ecological insights that help us answer some of engineering’s biggest questions.
path: A Flapping Good Model: Collaborative approaches to machine learning for biodiversity

items.title: Spatial is special
items.text: Invited talk at the British Cartographic Society Annual Conference 2024

items.title: Abstract
items.text:
Geospatial data plays an instrumental role in supporting projects in the engineering sector, enabling us to understand the world around us so that we can make better informed decisions, improve efficiencies, and drive digital innovation.

Our work at Mott MacDonald often involves wrangling complex datasets to answer multifaceted questions.

I talk through how Geography has played a role in shaping my expereinces today, and highlight three projects in Net Zero, Natural Asset Management and City Planning domains where we have used interesting ways to give life to spatial data and make an impact.

items.text: Download slides

items.title: Unlocking the power of geospatial with R
items.text: Presented at satRdays London 2024
path: Unlocking the power of geospatial with R

items.title: Abstract
items.text:
In this presentation, we will illustrate how we use R at Mott MacDonald to harness the power of geospatial data with two examples – Risk Modelling for Ash Dieback and Creative Geospatial Visualisation for Impactful Communication.

The Ash Dieback Pipeline is a computer vision project which attempts to identify trees with the Ash Dieback disease from video footage of roadways around the UK. We intend to showcase how we use R to process a variety of geospatial datasets and attempt to model the risk to road users associated with a diseased tree remaining untreated.

Our work at Mott MacDonald often involves wrangling complex datasets to answer multifaceted questions. R provides excellent toolkits for integrating, analysing, and visualising geospatial datasets.

We intend to demonstrate how R can be used for creative visualisation of geospatial data to extract and communicate actionable insights.

Through these examples, we hope to outline our team’s maturity journey towards building multilingual spatial data science capabilities alongside traditional GIS platforms.
path: Unlocking the power of geospatial with R
